## Revealed Bayesian Expected Utility with Limited Data

∗

John Rehbeck

Department of Economics The Ohio State University rehbeck.7@osu.edu

Last Updated:

May 21, 2021

## Abstract

This paper characterizes expected utility preferences with information acquisition and Bayesian updating using stochastic choice data that is not state-dependent. This contrasts with research that uses state-dependent stochastic choices which is difficult to obtain outside of the laboratory. The characterization is in the spirit of the Wald-Pearce lemma and requires that there is no random deviation rule that improves ex-ante expected utility for all possible information structures consistent with the data. We extend the result to place bounds on an unknown prior and facilitate welfare comparisons in the presence of framing. These bounds are computable via linear programming.

JEL Classification Numbers : D01; D80

Keywords : Limited Data; Bayesian Updating; Information Acquisition; Revealed Preference

∗ I thank Victor Aguiar, Roy Allen, Yaron Azreili, Christopher P. Chambers, Ce Liu, and Renkun Yang for useful comments. Any remaining errors are my own.

## 1 Introduction

This paper studies what can be learned about preferences from stochastic choice data of acts for an individual who acquires information, updates according to Bayes rule, and chooses according to expected utility for a given posterior. This is a limited dataset in that other work on revealed preference in the presence of information acquisition assumes a researcher has access to state-dependent stochastic choice data. However, in order to collect statedependent stochastic choice data, a researcher must be able to observe the action an individual chose and ex-post observe which state occurs. It is possible to gather state-dependent stochastic choice data in a laboratory setting since the researcher knows the true state of the world that obtains. However, state-dependent stochastic choice datasets may be difficult to acquire from field data.

To understand the difficulty in collecting state-dependent stochastic choice data in the field, consider a legal proceeding where a judge makes a decision to convict or acquit a defendant. In this example, the defendant is either innocent or guilty. The options of convict and acquit are often represented as two acts that the judge can choose, while innocent and guilty are thought of as states of the world. If a researcher wanted to use an existing revealed preference test of Bayesian expected utility, then they would need to collect state-dependent stochastic choice data. However, this would require that an individual knows how often a judge convicts and acquits conditional on whether the defendant is truly innocent or guilty. However, if one could know the true innocence or guilt of the defendant, then it would render the legal proceeding useless. A similar problem occurs when considering whether or not a consumer purchases an item when the quality can be high or low.

While collecting state-dependent stochastic choice data seems difficult for the examples above, collecting stochastic choice data that does not depend on the state is relatively easy to collect. For example, one can observe how often a judge convicts or acquits a defendant. Similarly, it is easy to observe how

often a consumer purchases an item. Therefore, when trying to use field data to test Bayesian expected utility maximization with information acquisition this is an important dataset to consider.

The first result (Theorem 1) gives a characterization of Bayesian expected utility with information acquisition from a single menu of acts using stochastic choice data that does not depend on the state, but when an individual's prior is known. The result shows that the problem can be characterized by linear programming and rests on a no-improving deviation argument. In particular, the argument is similar to the insight from Pearce [1984] and Bernheim [1984] that a strictly-dominated strategy is never a best-reply strategy. Next, we show how to extend the result to multiple menus when the prior is unknown (Theorem 2). Thus, a researcher can recover information on an individual's prior even when the state of the world cannot be verified after it occurs. 1 We also discuss how one can make welfare comparisons when the same decision problem is framed in different ways following Caplin and Martin [2018] even when one only observes stochastic choices.

For general cases, it is difficult to see how the the conditions in Theorem 1 place bounds on an individual's prior distribution and subsequent behavior even when the prior is known. Thus, we give explicit bounds for some special cases that may be of empirical interest. In particular, we focus on decision problems where there are either two acts or two states. When there are only two states and two acts, Proposition 1 gives a simple expression that bounds how often an individual can choose an act. A generalization of this result for two acts and many states follows immediately from Alonso and Cˆ amara [2016]. We also provide bounds on behavior when there are three acts and two states in Proposition 3 and Proposition 4 that shows how bounds can greatly vary depending on the location of the prior.

This paper is related to a series of papers looking at revealed preference characterizations of models with information. The work of Caplin and Martin

1 For example, whether the defendent is truly innocent or guilty is likely impossible to verify. The following quote from Shakur [1996] is relevant here:'Only God can judge me.'

[2015] is the closest research to this paper. In particular, Caplin and Martin [2015] study necessary and sufficient conditions for Bayesian expected utility with information acquisition using state-dependent stochastic choice data. The characterization in this paper differs by only using stochastic choice data and shows the relation to minimax properties. Thus, this paper is also related to the work of Geanakoplos [2013] that looks at the relation of the generalized axiom of revealed preferences and the minimax theorem.

The conditions that characterize the model are similar to no improving deviation arguments from Wald [1949] and Pearce [1984]. Similar intuition is also pointed out for dynamic choices in de Oliveira and Lamba [2019]. The work of Wald [1949] and de Oliveira and Lamba [2019] examine when an act or sequence of acts can be chosen from some information acquisition process. Pearce [1984] examines when a pure strategy can be rationalized by some beliefs. The work in this paper provides results when one wants to check if a specific distribution of chosen acts can be rationalized.

Other research on revealed preference and information acquisition using state-dependent stochastic choice datasets includes Caplin and Dean [2015], Chambers et al. [2018], Caplin and Martin [2018]. Caplin and Dean [2015] characterizes a model of costly information acquisition that generalizes the Shannon entropy cost function. The paper by Chambers et al. [2018] characterizes a general model of information acquisition that is monotone in ex-ante expected utility. Both the papers of Caplin and Dean [2015] and Chambers et al. [2018] assume a cost function that is consistent across decision problems. In contrast, the work of Caplin and Martin [2015] and this paper do not make this assumption. The paper by Caplin and Martin [2018] looks at conditions for information acquisition when there are different frames that affect the decision maker and when one can make welfare comparisons.

We provide a non-extensive review of other literature on information acquisition. There has been some work relating information acquisition and stochastic choice probabilities in Matˇ ejka and McKay [2015], Lu [2016], Mor-

ris and Strack [2017], and Lin [2018]. One notable paper is Lu [2016] which provides a characterization of random choice with unobserved information acquisition when prizes are the domain of lotteries using stochastic choice probabilities. There is additional work that examines information acquisition axiomatically such as Ergin and Sarver [2010], Denti et al. [2016], and Ellis [2018]. The methods we use to produce bounds on choice probabilities when there are two acts or two states are closely linked to the work on Bayesian persuasion studied in Kamenica and Gentzkow [2011]. In particular, we set up several artificial sender-receiver problems to place bounds on how often different acts are chosen for results in Section 4. There is also work by Bergemann and Morris [2016] that examines games with incomplete information and information structures.

The paper proceeds as follows. Section 2 provides definitions of the decision environment, a stochastic choice dataset, and a Bayesian expected utility maximizer. Section 3 provides the general revealed preference characterization, discusses conditions with an unknown prior, and discusses welfare bounds in the presence of framing. Section 4 provides characterizations when there are two states or two actions. Section 5 provides final remarks.

## 2 Definitions

This paper studies a decision maker facing actions with state-contingent payoffs who has expected utility and follows Bayesian updating. Let Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , . . . , ω L u be a finite set of states and let X denote a set of outcomes. Denote the set of all acts be X Ω . The set of all finite decision problems is denoted by A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t A GLYPH&lt;132&gt; X Ω | | A | GLYPH&lt;160&gt; 8u . For this analysis, it is possible that an individual collects different information for every set of acts.

A few more definitions are needed to define Bayesian expected utility maximization with information acquisition. Let a full support prior be given by µ P ∆ p Ω q and let the set of posteriors be denoted Γ GLYPH&lt;16&gt; ∆ p Ω q . 2 An arbitrary

2 For a finite set S , we denote the set of probability distributions over the elements in

posterior is denoted by γ P Γ. This paper considers information acquisition using information structures that are distributions with finite support over the set Γ. 3 An information structure induced by Bayes updating is required to have an expectation over posteriors that is equal to the prior distribution. The set of such information structures consistent with Bayesian updating is defined below.

Definition 1. Denote Π GLYPH&lt;132&gt; ∆ p Γ q as the set of information structures that have finite support and satisfy Bayesian updating for the prior µ . Formally, for any π P Π it follows that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where π p γ q is the probability of having posterior γ and Supp p π q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t γ P Γ | π p γ q ¡ 0 u .

With these definitions in hand, we now define a Bayesian expected utility (BEU) maximizer. A BEU maximizer is one who makes an optimal choice according to expected utility at each posterior from an information structure. It is possible for the individual to mix choices in the case of utility ties by introducing a choice function that depends on posteriors. In particular, the choice function for decision problem A is denoted C A pGLYPH&lt;4&gt; | γ q : A GLYPH&lt;209&gt; ∆ p A q and this allows the individual to randomize between acts.

Definition 2. For an information structure π P Π , an individual with choice function C A is a Bayesian expected utility maximizer (BEU maximizer) if for every γ P Supp p π q and for every a P A with C p a | γ q ¡ 0 , then for all b P A

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

a set by ∆ p S q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t p P R | S | GLYPH&lt;0&gt; | GLYPH&lt;176&gt; | S | s GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 p s GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 u . A full support prior has µ glyph[lscript] GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ p ω glyph[lscript] q ¡ 0 for all glyph[lscript] GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 , . . . , L .

3 Note that for every subjective signal, there is an associated information structure that represents the distribution of posteriors induced by the signaling device and Bayes updating.

For a BEU maximizer, there are no costs to acquire information. Moreover, the information structure the agent uses to make decisions is not required to be optimal according to the ex-ante expected utility. 4 However, the individual makes decisions according to the expected utility induced for any posterior distribution of the information structure. The fact that the acquired information may not be ex-ante optimal allows us to make welfare comparisons when the same decision problem is framed in two different ways as in Caplin and Martin [2018]. We discuss welfare comparisons in Section 3.3.

For the situation with a single decision problem, we first assume that the prior beliefs of the decision maker µ P ∆ p Ω q are known. That the prior is known is often assumed in experiments since the experimenter knows how likely each state occurs. In this case, the individual's prior is often taken as the true distribution of states. When one wants to use a subjective prior, there are numerous methods to elicit beliefs. 5 This assumption allows us to create interesting and interpretable bounds on choice probabilities for simple examples in Section 4. In particular, we give bounds for menus with two acts and any number of states and bounds on three acts with two states. These bounds may be of interest to experimentalists since most experiments have either small state spaces or few acts.

After examining the case of a known prior and single menu, we consider when the prior is unknown and the researcher has information from possibly many decision problems. This case more closely resembles the data that is collected from the field. We show that the main insights from the single decision problem carry over and place tight restrictions on an individual's prior distribution. In particular, we show in Example 1 that information from two decision problems with two acts and two states can refute the existance of any BEU maximizer.

4 The ex-ante expected utility for the information structure π P Π for decision problem A P A is given by GLYPH&lt;176&gt; γ P Supp p π q π p γ q max a P A ! GLYPH&lt;176&gt; L glyph[lscript] GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 γ p ω glyph[lscript] q u p a p ω glyph[lscript] qq ) . Thus, an individual may have information which does not optimize this value.

5 See Morgan et al. [1990] for a textbook treatment on eliciting subjective beliefs.

The paper assumes that the utility function u : X GLYPH&lt;209&gt; R is known. This is a restrictive assumption, but it is common in the literature. 6 For example, when the prize space X GLYPH&lt;16&gt; R GLYPH&lt;0&gt; is money won in an experiment, the researcher may use u p x q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; x to examine expected value maximization or impose restrictions from constant absolute risk aversion (CARA) or constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) utility. When the utilities are parameterized like CARA or CRRA, the results here will place bounds on parameter values that are consistent with the data.

Unlike the previous work on information acquisition and revealed preference, this paper examines a stochastic choice dataset that does not depend on the state. We give the formal definition below.

Definition 3. A stochastic choice dataset for A P A is a collection of choice probabilities of acts given by t P A p a qu a P A . Here P A p a q is the probability act a is chosen from decision problem A and it follows that GLYPH&lt;176&gt; a P A P A p a q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 .

A stochastic choice dataset can be obtained when the state cannot be verified to have occured. Even if one has state-dependent stochastic choice data, the stochastic choice dataset may be more amenable in applications when there are only a few observations conditional on each state. For the case of state-dependent stochastic choice data, the relation to BEU was worked out by Caplin and Martin [2015]. We now present the definition of Bayesian expected utility maximization for a stochastic choice dataset.

Definition 4. Given a prior µ P ∆ p Ω q , utility function u : X GLYPH&lt;209&gt; R , and a decision problem A P A , the stochastic choice dataset t P A p a qu a P A is rationalized by a Bayes expected utility (BEU) maximizer if there exists an information structure π P Π and a choice function C A : Supp p π q GLYPH&lt;209&gt; ∆ p A q that satisfies

6 If the utility function is completely unrestricted, then complete indifference can always rationalize the data. However, when there are monotonicity restrictions or a set of hypothesized utility functions one can generate restrictions. This is discussed in Chambers et al. [2018].

1. Maximization: For all γ P Supp p π q and for all a P A with C p a | γ q ¡ 0 , then for all b P A

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

2. Data matching: For all a P A

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Here the definition of Bayes updating is embeded in the assumption π P Π. Unlike the works of Caplin and Martin [2015], Caplin and Dean [2015], Chambers et al. [2018], and Caplin and Martin [2018], information on the posteriors in the information structure is not revealed from the data since we do not observe state-dependent stochastic choice data.

## 3 Main Results

We first present results for the single decision problem with a known prior. Following this result, we describe a similar intuition for multiple decision problems when there is an unknown prior distribution. Lastly, we discuss how to use the results to evaluate welfare changes in the presence of framing.

## 3.1 Single Decision Problem

This section presents the general conditions for a stochastic choice dataset to be rationalized by BEU for a single decision problem and known prior µ P ∆ p Ω q . When dealing with stochastic choice data, one can begin by naively guessing that each chosen act has a posterior distribution that recommends the choice of the a th act and this posterior occurs with probability P A p a q . This guess facilitates a system of linear equalities and inequalities where the posteriors that recommend each action are unknown. This is similar in spirit

to the inequalities studied in Afriat [1967] and Varian [1982]. Following intuition from Geanakoplos [2013] we also give a result that follows from minimax arguments.

This result depends on the acts that are chosen with positive probability. Let Supp p P A q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a P A | P A p a q ¡ 0 u denote the set of acts chosen with positive probability from decision problem A . We also use the concept of a deviation rule as discussed in Wald [1949], Bernheim [1984], and Pearce [1984] that allows an individual to change their action at each posterior. In particular, let the set D GLYPH&lt;16&gt; A | Supp p P A q| be the set of deviations for each chosen action and let ν P ∆ p A q | Supp p P A q| be a lottery over the deviations for each chosen act. Here we say ν a P ∆ p A q is a deviating lottery for the a th chosen act. Thus, ν a p d q is the probability of deviating to d P A when the a th act is chosen.

Theorem 1. Given a prior µ P ∆ p Ω q , utility function u : X GLYPH&lt;209&gt; R , and decision problem A P A . For a dataset of stochastic choices t P A p a qu a P A the following are equivalent:

- (i) t P A p a qu a P A is rationalized by a BEU maximizer.
- (ii) For all a P Supp p P A q there exists a posterior γ a P ∆ p Ω q such that for all b P A

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Moreover, the collection of posteriors t γ a u a P Supp p P A q satisfies

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

(iii) There is no random deviation rule ν P ∆ p A q | Supp p P A q| such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

holds for every collection of posteriors t γ a u a P Supp p P A q with GLYPH&lt;176&gt; a P Supp p P A q P A p a q γ a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ .

All proofs are collected in Appendix A. The statement of (i) is the definition of a BEU maximizer. The statement of (ii) provides a system of linear inequalities that check whether there exists a BEU maximizer. The statement of (ii) is not simply a restatement of (i) since it shows that one only needs to find as many unknown posteriors as there are chosen acts. This is similar to arguments using Carath´ eodory's theorem (See for example Rockafellar [2015]) to obtain tractable information structures in applied problems. Finally, the statement of (iii) follows from a minimax argument and rules out the existence of a random deviation rule that improves utility for all posteriors consistent with Bayesian updating. 7 This condition is similar to the conditions of Wald [1949] and Pearce [1984] that rules out the existence of a mixed strategy that dominates the chosen act. Similar intuition is pointed out in the study of dynamic choices in de Oliveira and Lamba [2019].

The condition in Theorem 1(iii) can be easily used to show that if there is a ˜ a P A that is state-wise dominated, then it must never be chosen. Since ˜ a is state-wise dominated there exists some b P A with b GLYPH&lt;24&gt; ˜ a such that for all glyph[lscript] P t 1 , . . . , L u it follows that u p ˜ a p ω glyph[lscript] qq GLYPH&lt;160&gt; u p b p ω glyph[lscript] qq . In this case, it cannot be that P A p ˜ a q ¡ 0. If this was the case, one could choose ν ˜ a p b q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 and for all a P Supp p P A q such that a GLYPH&lt;24&gt; ˜ a let ν a p a q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1. This is a deviation rule that switches to b when ˜ a was chosen and matches all other choices. This rule violates Theorem 1(iii) so BEU maximization is refuted. This is essentially the Wald-Pearce lemma [Wald, 1949, Pearce, 1984] and we record this as a corollary below.

7 A previous version of this paper had a linear programming dual characterization that is similar in spirit to the result of Chambers and Rehbeck [2019].

Corollary 1. For a set of acts A P A , if for ˜ a P A if there exists b P A such that b GLYPH&lt;24&gt; ˜ a and for all glyph[lscript] P t 1 , . . . , L u that u p b p ω glyph[lscript] qq GLYPH&lt;1&gt; u p ˜ a p ω glyph[lscript] qq ¡ 0 , then a BEU maximizer must have P A p ˜ a q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0 .

This is a restriction of BEU that is true regardless of an individual's prior distribution. Therefore, BEU with information acquisition is always refuted when a state-wise dominated act is chosen. When the set of prizes X is given by money, the researcher may be unwilling to give numbers to the utility functions, but may be willing to assume more money gives more utility. In this case even though the utility numbers are unknown, the BEU model will require that individuals do not pick acts that state-wise give less money. Thus, for reasonable prize spaces BEU maximization can still be refuted with limited knowledge of the utility function.

## 3.2 Multiple Menus and Unknown Prior

When there is no information on the prior, we can still build on the earlier characterization. Let D GLYPH&lt;128&gt; A be a collection of decision problems. Where each A P D has known stochastic choice probabilities P A . Thus, the dataset of interest is given by t P A u A P D . Let the unknown prior distribution be given by ˆ µ P ∆ p Ω q . The characterization of choices with an unknown prior follows similar arguments from Theorem 1, but we now need to define a larger space of possible deviation rules. To this end, let D D GLYPH&lt;16&gt; -A P D A | Supp p P A q| so that we consider deviations possible for every chosen act. Similarly, ν GLYPH&lt;16&gt; p ν A q A P D P -A P D ∆ p A q | Supp p P A q| is all lotteries of deviations In particular, for all A P D the element ν A P ∆ p A q | Supp p P A q| contains all deviation rules for menu A . Thus, for all A P D and a P Supp p A q the lottery ν A a P A is the deviation lottery for the a th act in menu A .

Theorem 2. Given a utility function u : X GLYPH&lt;209&gt; R and a collection of decision problems D GLYPH&lt;128&gt; A . For a dataset t P A u A P D the following are equivalent:

- (i) For every A P D the stochastic choices P A are rationalized by a BEU maximizer with common prior ˆ µ P ∆ p Ω q .
- (ii) For all A P D and for all a P Supp p P A q there exists a posterior γ A a P ∆ p Ω q such that for all b P A

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Moreover, there exists ˆ µ P ∆ p Ω q such that for all A P D the collection of posteriors t γ A a u a P Supp p P A q satisfies

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

- (iii) There is no random deviation rule ν P -A P D ∆ p A q | Supp p P A q| such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

holds for every collection of posteriors tt γ A a u a P Supp p P A q u A P D and priors ˆ µ P ∆ p Ω q with GLYPH&lt;176&gt; a P Supp p P A q P A p a q γ A a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; ˆ µ .

The characterization from Theorem 2(iii) is a simple extension of Theorem 1(iii) since the set of restrictions over priors and posteriors is a compact convex set and the utility from each decision problem can be compared to one another since the utility is linear. However, there are two main differences. First, the set of deviation rules grows when there are more menus. Second, the deviation rule must be stronger since it must hold for any possible prior distribution.

One may be concerned that the restriction above will not place meaningful bounds on prior distributions. In the following example, we show that even a single decision problem is informative about the prior distribution even with deterministic choices. Moreover, we show when there are choices from

two decision problems that the bounds on the prior may be inconsistent. This says that there are datasets that can refute BEU maximization even without knowledge of the prior that are not simply resulting from state-wise domination of the utility function.

Example 1. Suppose an individual faces decisions when Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , ω 2 u where ˆ µ p ω 2 q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; ˆ µ and ˆ µ p ω 1 q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 GLYPH&lt;1&gt; ˆ µ are unknown, and faces decision problems A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b u and A 1 GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a 1 , b 1 u where the utility in each state is given by

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where P A p b q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 and P A 1 p b 1 q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0 . For act b to be chosen with P A p b q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 from A assuming BEU maximization, it follows that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which simplifies to ˆ µ ¥ 1 4 . Similarly for act a 1 to be chosen with P A 1 p a 1 q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 from A 1 assuming BEU maximization, which requires that ˆ µ / 1 11 . Since the first and second bound are inconsistent, this dataset refutes BEU maximization.

## 3.3 Robust Welfare Comparisons

We now describe how one can make welfare comparisons in the presence of framing effects similar to Caplin and Martin [2018]. Let F be a set of frames in the spirit of Salant and Rubinstein [2008] and Bernheim and Rangel [2009]. The pairs p A, f q , p A, g q P A GLYPH&lt;2&gt; F gives the decision problem A posed with frames f and g where f GLYPH&lt;24&gt; g . To each framed decision problem, we assume there is a stochastic choice dataset so t P A,f p a qu a P A gives the stochastic choice probabilities when A has frame f and t P A,g p a qu a P A gives the stochastic choice

probabilities when A has frame g .

As mentioned earlier, the stochastic choices from a decision problem are not necessarily ex-ante optimal in any sense. This means that if the decision problem is framed two different ways, then it is possible for one frame to give a higher ex-ante expected utility. In this case, a researcher could conclude the individual is better off at a given frame. To see this, let Supp p P A,f q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a P A | P A,f p a q ¡ 0 u be the set of acts chosen in frame f . Similarly, let Supp p P A,g q be the set of acts chosen in frame g . We let E γ a f r u p a f qs GLYPH&lt;16&gt; GLYPH&lt;176&gt; L glyph[lscript] GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 γ a f p ω glyph[lscript] q u p a f p ω glyph[lscript] qq and E γ ag r u p a g qs GLYPH&lt;16&gt; GLYPH&lt;176&gt; L glyph[lscript] GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 γ a g p ω glyph[lscript] q u p a g p ω glyph[lscript] qq .

Now, one can conclude that frame f induces better choices than frame g if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

for all posteriors γ a f and γ a g consistent with the restrictions of Theorem 1(ii) when the prior is known.

When the prior is unknown as in Theorem 2, there are two possible cases to check. First, one could check if there is a prior that such that one could improve an individual's choices. Alternatively, one could require the stronger statement that the difference is positive for all possible prior distributions. These comparisons can naturally be checked when there are many menus and frames using the additional restrictions from Theorem 2(ii) by taking the max and min of the difference of utility in Equation 1. One could also use the restrictions above for menus framed two different ways to check consisteny of the prior across frames. This would allow a researcher to examine if individual priors are independent of the frame. All of these questions can be posed as linear programming problems and are readily computable.

## 4 Conditions for Simple Decision Problems

In this section, we consider the revealed preference conditions when there are only two actions A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b u or two states Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , ω 2 u when the prior µ P ∆ p Ω q is known. For these simple cases, one can get conditions that completely characterize behavior without resorting to deviation rules. These may be informative since they give a basic understanding of the geometry of the problem and show why it is difficult to remove all the unknowns in Theorem 1 and Theorem 2. In particular, we show the bounds can drastically change depending on the location of the prior when there are three acts and two states.

## 4.1 Two Actions

First, consider the simplest case when A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b u and Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , ω 2 u . For this problem, it is without loss to consider all of the results in terms of a single term µ to represent the prior probability. Let the prior be denoted by µ p ω 2 q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ and µ p ω 1 q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 GLYPH&lt;1&gt; µ . Before going further, we highlight the act that would get chosen in the presence of no information. We present the following definition for a general set A P A since we use this definition when there are three acts in Section 4.2. If an action a P A satisfies

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

we say this action a is a default option. We note that if a and b are both default options, then without acquiring information the individual is indifferent and any stochastic choices are consistent with BEU maximization. In fact for any number of actions and states, if all acts are default, then there are no restrictions on behavior.

For there to be any restrictions on behavior for two actions and two states, we require the notion of a strict default option. If an action a P A satisfies

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

we say action a is a strict default option. For the rest of the results, we assume that a is the strict default option.

The result that characterizes necessary and sufficient conditions depends on the differences of utility for acts a and b in each state. Thus, for all glyph[lscript] P t 1 , 2 u let ∆ a,b u p ω glyph[lscript] q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; u p a p ω glyph[lscript] qq GLYPH&lt;1&gt; u p b p ω glyph[lscript] qq and note that ∆ b,a u p ω glyph[lscript] q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; GLYPH&lt;1&gt; ∆ a,b u p ω glyph[lscript] q . Moreover, recall that ∆ a,b u p ω glyph[lscript] q ¥ 0 for some state for act a to ever be chosen when A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b u by Corollary 1. We now provide a characterization on behavior when there are two acts and two states.

Proposition 1. Given Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , ω 2 u , a prior µ P p 0 , 1 q , u : X GLYPH&lt;209&gt; R , and the decision problem A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b u with strict default option a and ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q ¡ 0 , the stochastic choice dataset t P A p a q , P A p b qu is rationalized by a BEU maximizer if and only if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

The condition is only on the choice probability of act b , but this condition also bounds the smallest probability of choosing act a since P A p a qGLYPH&lt;0&gt; P A p b q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1. We discuss the intuition for Proposition 1 below.

First, since a is a strict default option, it is possible that b is never chosen. Thus, there is no lower bound on the probability of choosing b . Now one only needs to look for the largest probability that b could be chosen using some information structure. However, this can be found by solving a Bayesian persuasion game when there is a sender who only receives positive utility when act b is chosen. Note that b can be chosen when the expected utility is equal to that of act a . This occurs where the lines representing the expected utility of acts a and b intersect in Figure 1.

First, note the posterior can be represented by a single number since there are two states. We let γ GLYPH&lt;16&gt; γ p ω 2 q where 1 GLYPH&lt;1&gt; γ GLYPH&lt;16&gt; γ p ω 1 q by complementary probability. Thus, the closest posterior to the prior where b can be chosen is given by ¯ γ b in Figure 1. The most that this posterior can be induced by an information structure occurs by recommending act a with the posterior ¯ γ a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0 and

receiving the posterior ¯ γ b with probability µ { ¯ γ b . Any probability of choosing act b less than the maximum is induced by an information structure that randomizes between the information structure which maximizes the probability of choosing act b and the prior.

Figure 1: Example of Proposition 1

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

Now consider when there are two acts and more than two states. Suppose the decision problem consists of two acts A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b u and Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , . . . , ω L u where L ¡ 2. We assume that act a is a strict default under the prior. Thus, the maximal probability of choosing act a is one. However, the maximal probability of b is bounded above. Again, we look at the Bayesian persuasion problem where the sender is trying to maximize P A p b q . However, this problem was solved in Alonso and Cˆ amara [2016].

Before stating the proposition, we require the following definitions. Let the set of beliefs where the agent chooses b be denoted

B GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t γ P Γ | GLYPH&lt;176&gt; L glyph[lscript] GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 γ p ω glyph[lscript] q ∆ b,a u p ω glyph[lscript] q ¥ 0 u . Let the states where b is weakly better than a be given by Ω b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω P Ω | ∆ b,a u p ω q ¥ 0 u . Assign the set of strong rejection beliefs that assign zero probability to any state where b is optimal as R GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t γ P Γ | ω P Ω b æ γ p ω q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0 u .

Proposition 2. Given Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , ω 2 , . . . , ω L u , a full support prior µ P ∆ p Ω q , u : X GLYPH&lt;209&gt; R , and the decision problem A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b u with strict default option

a and where act b is not state-wise dominated, the stochastic choice dataset t P A p a q , P A p b qu is rationalized by a BEU maximizer if and only if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Where d p µ, γ q is the Euclidean distance between the prior belief and posterior γ and γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; GLYPH&lt;1&gt; , γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; GLYPH&lt;0&gt; solve

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

subject to γ GLYPH&lt;1&gt; P R , γ GLYPH&lt;0&gt; P B and t γ GLYPH&lt;1&gt; , µ, γ GLYPH&lt;0&gt; u are collinear.

## 4.2 Three Acts and Two States

We now examine when the decision problem consists of three acts A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b, c u and two states Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , ω 2 u . Again, the prior is such that µ p ω 1 q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 GLYPH&lt;1&gt; µ and µ p ω 2 q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ . We also assume that act a is a strict default option. This section demonstrates the difficulty of obtaining general bounds like Proposition 1 for more complicated environments. In particular, one must consider how the prior and utility differences interact when trying to find bounds on behavior.

For the case of three acts and two states, we note that there are essentially two cases to consider: (i) When the strict default a is 'in-between' acts b and c and (ii) when the strict default a is on one side of b and c . These are represented in Figure 2.

Figure 2: Cases for Three Acts

![Image](data:image/png;base64,)

To find the bounds for the two cases above, we apply the same reasoning as Proposition 1 and look for the maximal probability each act can be chosen for various sender-receiver problems. However, for the case of three acts, one needs to determine what other actions are chosen when looking for the maximum choice probability. For example, in case (i) one can look to maximize the probability of choosing c when the remaining probability is assigned to a or b .

Once one finds these extreme points, any convex combination of these probabilities can be induced by randomizing between the information structures that induce the extreme choices. Thus, the restrictions on the choice probabilities require that they are in the convex hull of these extreme points. The notation conv p S q for a set S GLYPH&lt;132&gt; R J denotes the convex hull of S which is the set of all convex combinations of points in S .

To simplify the conditions for the case of Figure 2(i), we define the coordinates where the expected utility of the acts intersects. In particular, let

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where ¯ γ a,c is the posterior where the expected utility of act a and c are equal and ¯ γ a,b is the posterior where the expected utility of act a and b are equal.

The bounds on behavior for the case of Figure 2(i) are given in the proposition below.

Proposition 3. Given Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , ω 2 u , a prior µ P p 0 , 1 q , a utility function u : X GLYPH&lt;209&gt; R , and the decision problem A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b, c u with strict default option a and utility differences ∆ c,a u p ω 1 q , ∆ c,b u p ω 1 q , ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q , ∆ b,c u p ω 2 q ¡ 0 , the stochastic choice dataset given by t P A p a q , P A p b q , P A p c qu is a BEU maximizer if and only if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

There is a similar result for the case of Figure 2(ii). For this case, we assume that there are some posteriors where act b can be chosen, otherwise the problem reduces to the case of Proposition 1. In this case, the only way to maximize the choice of act b or act c is to also choose act a with positive probability. These restrictions on behavior are intuitively stronger since there is no way to choose only acts b and c with positive probability. To simplify the conditions for the case of Figure 2(ii), we define the coordinates where the expected utility of the acts intersects. In particular, let

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where ˜ γ a,b is the posterior where the expected utility of act a and b are equal and ˜ γ b,c is the posterior where the expected utility of act b and c are equal. The bounds on behavior for the case of Figure 2(ii) are given in the proposition below.

Proposition 4. Given Ω GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t ω 1 , ω 2 u , a prior µ P p 0 , 1 q , a utility function u : X GLYPH&lt;209&gt; R , and decision problem A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a, b, c u with strict default action a , action b that is not dominated at every posterior, and utility differences ∆ a,c u p ω 1 q , ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q , ∆ c,a u p ω 2 q , ∆ c,b u p ω 2 q ¡ 0 , the stochastic choice dataset

given by t P A p a q , P A p b q , P A p c qu is a BEU maximizer if and only if

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

## 5 Discussion

This paper provides necessary and sufficient conditions for Bayesian expected utility maximization when a researcher only has a stochastic choice dataset that does not depend on the state. In particular, there can be no random deviation rules that improve expected utility for all possible information structures consistent with the dataset. We argue that state independent stochastic choice data may be more natural when analyzing information acquisition from the field when it is difficult to verify the occurance of a state. This characterization can also be used to place bounds on the prior distribution by using only stochastic choice probabilities. Moreover, one can make welfare comparisons in the spirit of Caplin and Martin [2018] using only stochastic choice data of acts even when the prior is unknown. We also provide some simple bounds on behavior for decision problems that involve only two states or two acts to show that the bounds are non-trivial for common settings, provide intuition, and show difficulties of removing the unknowns in the characterization with deviation rules.

## Appendix A Proofs

Proof of Theorem 1. ( p i q øæ p ii q ) Let π be an information structure from a BEU maximizer. For all a P A with P A p a q ¡ 0, let G a be the set of posteriors ˆ γ P Supp p π q where a is chosen. Note that π p ˆ γ q is the probability that posterior ˆ γ occurs from information structure π . We now generate an appropriate information structure that satisfies (ii).

Let γ a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; GLYPH&lt;176&gt; γ P G a π p γ q C p a | γ q GLYPH&lt;176&gt; γ 1 P Ga π p γ 1 q C p a | γ 1 q γ and note that γ a is a posterior since it is a convex combination of posteriors. Moreover, a is a maximal element according to expected utility since it was maximal for each γ P G a . Choose the information structure so that the probability of γ a is ˜ π p γ a q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; GLYPH&lt;176&gt; γ P G a π p γ q C p a | γ q . Note ˜ π is a valid information structure and ˜ π p γ a q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; P A p a q by construction. Thus, GLYPH&lt;176&gt; a P Supp p P A q ˜ π p γ a q γ a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; GLYPH&lt;176&gt; a P Supp p P A q P A p a q γ a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ so the second equality holds.

pp ii q øæ p i qq Suppose (ii) holds. Let G GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t γ a u a P Supp p P A q be the set of all posteriors generated from the set of linear inequalities where Supp p P A q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; t a P A | P A p a q ¡ 0 u . This allows the possibility that γ a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; γ b for some a, b P Supp p P A q . If γ a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; γ b , then it must be that the expected utilities are equal using the pair of weak inequalities in (ii). Therefore, construct the information structure π P Π over γ P G as

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

This π is an information structure by the second equality in (ii). Finally, for all γ P G set

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

.

It follows that P A p a q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; π p γ a q C A p a | γ a q which shows that the data is matched. Maximization follows from the construction of the posteriors and the first set of inequalities.

pp ii q GLYPH&lt;244&gt; p iii qq We prove this using a minimax theorem (See Du and Pardalos [2013] for additional information on minimax theorems). First, we define the set of posteriors for a given prior µ P ∆ p Ω q of

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where γ A GLYPH&lt;16&gt; p γ A a q a P Supp p P A q collects posteriors for each act. Note that ∆ p Ω q is convex, closed, and bounded for each vector γ a . Moreover, the restriction GLYPH&lt;176&gt; a P Supp p P A q γ a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ on the vector of γ A is a closed convex set since it is an intersection of convex sets. Thus, Γ A is a convex and compact set.

The set of deviation lotteries ∆ p A q | Supp p P A q| is convex and compact by definition. Therefore, it follows that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

since the set Γ A and ∆ p A q | Supp p P A q| are compact convex sets, the objective is linear in γ A for each η , and the objective is linear in η for each γ A .

Suppose there exists posteriors given by ˜ γ A that satisfy the constraints in Theorem 1(ii). Thus, ˜ γ A P Γ A . It follows that for any η P ∆ p A q | Supp p P A q| that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Thus, there is no ν P ∆ p A q | Supp p P A q| that decreases utility for all posteriors in Γ A .

Now we prove (iii) implies (ii) by contrapositive that not (ii) implies not (iii). In otherwords, we want to show there is a random deviation rule that insures an improvement of utility when the system in (ii) fails. If there is no posterior such that (ii) holds, then for every posterior γ A P Γ A there exists an act ˜ a P Supp p P A q and ˜ b P A with ˜ b GLYPH&lt;24&gt; a such that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

However, this means that one can choose the deviation rule so that η ˜ a p ˜ b q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 and for all a P Supp p P A q with a GLYPH&lt;24&gt; ˜ a set η a p a q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 then for every γ A P Γ A it follows that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Thus,

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Applying the minimax result gives that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

so that any minimizeris a random deviation rule that can make the individual better off.

Proof of Theorem 2. ((i) GLYPH&lt;244&gt; (ii)) follows immediately from the equivalence of (i) and (ii) from Theorem 1 for each A P D and the common prior assumption.

pp ii q GLYPH&lt;244&gt; p iii qq This also follows from minimax arguments. First, let N GLYPH&lt;16&gt; -A P D | Supp p P A q| . Note that the set

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

where γ D GLYPH&lt;16&gt; p γ A q A P D gives the posteriors for each menu in D . Note that this is an intersection of probability simplices that are closed convex and bounded with the hyperplanes defined by Bayesian updating. Thus, Γ D is a compact convex set and the minimax theorem can be appled. The rest of the proof follows by repeating the arguments from Theorem 1(iii) and accounting for

every menu in D .

Proof of Proposition 1. Since a is the strict default, we know that a can get chosen with probability one at the prior. Moreover, since ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q ¡ 0, if a is the strict default then it must be that ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q ¡ 0 otherwise a would be state-wise dominated. Since P A p a q GLYPH&lt;0&gt; P A p b q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 it suffices to find an upper bound on P A p b q to describe all the restrictions of the theory. In particular, consider the game of Bayesian persuasion when the receiver has expected utility preferences using the known utility index numbers, while the receiver wants to maximize the probability that P A p b q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; p is chosen.

First, we can restrict looking at straightforward posteriors (See Kamenica and Gentzkow [2011]) γ a and γ b that recommend the receiver takes action a or action b respectively. Note action b is most prefered by the receiver when

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

so that for γ b to recommend b it must be that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Moreover, we note that µ GLYPH&lt;160&gt; γ b since a is a strict default. Similarly we must have γ a GLYPH&lt;160&gt; µ to consider finding a Bayesian plausible information structure. For given γ a and γ b that satisfy the constraints, the sender solves the problem

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Consider a fixed γ a and γ b that satisfy the constraints. Let p GLYPH&lt;6&gt; p γ a , γ b q be a

function of how often act b is chosen as a function of γ a and γ b that satisfy the constraints. Here we see that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

We note that p GLYPH&lt;6&gt; is strictly decreasing in γ a and strictly decreasing in γ b since

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

since γ a GLYPH&lt;160&gt; µ GLYPH&lt;160&gt; γ b . Since these are strictly decreasing, the maximum occurs when γ a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0 and γ b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q ∆ a,b u p ω 1 qGLYPH&lt;0&gt; ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q . Thus, we have that p GLYPH&lt;6&gt; GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ γ b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ GLYPH&lt;0&gt; µ ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q . Thus, it follows that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

since b can be chosen less often by mixing the optimal information structure with the prior µ .

Proof of Proposition 2. Proposition 1 of Alonso and Cˆ amara [2016] gives that this is the maximal probability of choosing action b . Any mixture of this information structure with the prior can induce choices with the probability of choosing b less than d p µ,γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; GLYPH&lt;1&gt; q d p µ,γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; GLYPH&lt;0&gt; qGLYPH&lt;0&gt; d p µ,γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; GLYPH&lt;1&gt; q .

Proof of Proposition 3. Since a is the strict default, we know that a can get chosen with probability one at the prior. We proceed by cases to find the limits to how often different actions can be chosen. In particular, we look for the largest probability each action can be chosen when unrestricted and when one is restricted to choosing some other action.

(Case i) We consider the maximal choice of b without any constraints. Moreover, since ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q , ∆ b,c u p ω 2 q ¡ 0 and ∆ c,a u p ω 1 q , ∆ c,b u p ω 1 q ¡ 0, if a is the strict default then it must be that ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q ¡ 0 and ∆ a,c u p ω 2 q ¡ 0 otherwise a would be strictly dominated. We now consider the game of

Bayesian persuasion when the receiver has expected utility preferences using the known utility index numbers, while the receiver wants to maximize the probability that P A p b q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; p b is chosen. Let ¯ γ a,b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q ∆ a,b u p ω 1 qGLYPH&lt;0&gt; ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q and ¯ γ a,c GLYPH&lt;16&gt; ∆ c,a u p ω 1 q ∆ c,a u p ω 1 qGLYPH&lt;0&gt; ∆ a,c u p ω 2 q .

First, we can restrict looking at straightforward posteriors (cf. Kamenica and Gentzkow [2011]) γ a , γ b , and γ c that recommend the receiver takes action a , b , or c respectively. Since a is a strict default and assumptions on utility difference, it follows that γ b ¡ µ and that one only needs to look at when action b dominates a for it to be chosen. These are the same conditions from Proposition 1 so that for γ b to recommend b it must be that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Moreover, since a is a strict default and by assumptions on utility differences, it follows that γ c GLYPH&lt;160&gt; µ and that one only needs to look at when action c dominates a for it to be chosen. Thus, we look for when

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

or equivalently

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which is between zero and one.

For given γ a , γ b , and γ c that satisfy the constraints, the sender solves the

problem

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Consider fixed γ a , γ b , and γ c that satisfy constraints so that when when p b GLYPH&lt;0&gt; p c GLYPH&lt;160&gt; 1, then Bayesian updating implies

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

and when p b GLYPH&lt;0&gt; p c GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1, then Bayesian updating implies

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

For both of these cases as long as p c GLYPH&lt;24&gt; 0, p b increases as γ c decreases, so γ c will bind at a minimum of γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; c GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0. Note that all γ a ¡ 0so that when p b GLYPH&lt;0&gt; p c GLYPH&lt;160&gt; 1, then p b can be chosen more often by choosing p c . Thus, it must be that p b GLYPH&lt;0&gt; p c GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1.

However, when p b GLYPH&lt;0&gt; p c GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 1 and γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; c GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0, it follows that p b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ γ b which is maximized when γ b is minimized. Thus, we have a tentative solution of p GLYPH&lt;6&gt; b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ ¯ γ a,b .

However, we have not ruled out the possibility that p c GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0. We consider

this in case (ii) below, and find the maximal probability of choosing b is given by p b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ GLYPH&lt;1&gt; ¯ γ a,c ¯ γ a,b GLYPH&lt;1&gt; γ a,c . However, we note that µ ¯ γ a,b ¡ µ GLYPH&lt;1&gt; ¯ γ a,c ¯ γ a,b GLYPH&lt;1&gt; γ a,c since µ GLYPH&lt;160&gt; ¯ γ a,b by definition. So the optimal choice of b is p GLYPH&lt;6&gt; b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ ¯ γ a,b . Thus, the point

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

has the maximum probability that act b can be chosen when expanding terms.

(Case ii) We look at the largest probability of choosing b if the agent is restricted to choosing a with the remaining probability. This is solved by the following problem

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Similar to Proposition 1, we find that the optimal p GLYPH&lt;6&gt; p γ a , γ b q are strictly decreasing in γ a and γ b so the constraints bind. Thus, γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; ∆ c,a u p ω 1 q ∆ c,a u p ω 1 qGLYPH&lt;0&gt; ∆ a,c u p ω 2 q and γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q ∆ a,b u p ω 1 qGLYPH&lt;0&gt; ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q so that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Thus, the point

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

has the maximum probability that action b can be chosen with the remaining probability going to a .

(Case iii) We look at the largest probability that c can be chosen without any constraints. This problem is symmetric to Case i and the arguments are omitted. Similar to Case i, this has all probability between actions c and b . The point

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

has the maximum probability that action c can be chosen.

(Case iv) We look at the largest probability that c can be chosen with the remaining probability constrained to go to action a . This problem is symmetric to Case ii and the arguments are omitted. The point

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

has the maximum probability that action c can be chosen with the remaining probability going to a .

Finally, we note that any convex combination of these choice probabilities can be induced by mixing the information structures used in these problems.

Any other choice probability necessarily has a choice probability that is greater than either the unconstrained or constrained problem. Thus, this gives a complete description of behavior consistent with Bayesian expected utility.

Proof of Proposition 4. Since a is the strict default, we know that a can get chosen with probability one at the prior. Moreover, since

∆ a,c u p ω 1 q , ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q , ∆ c,a u p ω 2 q , ∆ c,b u p ω 2 q ¡ 0 and b is not strictly dominated, we know there exist points where the expected utility of b gives the (weakly) highest utility and that ∆ b,c u p ω 1 q ¡ 0. Let ˜ γ a,b be the point where the expected utility of a and b are equal and ˜ γ b,c be the point where the expected utility of c and b are equal. From the conditions on utility differences and since b is not strictly dominated it follows that ˜ γ b,c ¥ ˜ γ a,b ¡ µ . Thus, the individual must choose a with some probability when choosing either b or c .

(Case i) We consider the maximal choice of b withut any constraints. To find this, consider the game of Bayesian persuasion when the receiver has expected utility preferences using the known utility index numbers, while the receiver wants to maximize the probability that P A p b q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; p b is chosen.

First, we can restrict looking at straightforward posteriors (cf. Kamenica and Gentzkow [2011]) γ a , γ b , and γ c that recommend the receiver takes action a , b , or c respectively. We need to look at when action b dominates a and c for it to be chosen. Rearranging these optimality conditions we get that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

and

Note the second term that bounds b also gives a lower bound on γ c .

To maximize the probability of b the sender solves the problem

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Consider a fixed γ a , γ b , and γ c that satisfy the constraints and p c a given probability that satisfies Bayesian updating, note when p b GLYPH&lt;0&gt; p c GLYPH&lt;160&gt; 1 which is necessary to choose b that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

Looking at how p b changes with γ a and holding p c fixed, we see that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

which is strictly decreasing so an optimal choice for the sender has γ GLYPH&lt;6&gt; a GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0.

Substituting in this relation, we see that

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

so that p b is strictly decreasing in p c . Thus, to maximize p b it must be that p c GLYPH&lt;16&gt; 0. Finally, p b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ γ b which is decreasing in γ b . Thus, we obtain that the maximal choice of act b is p GLYPH&lt;6&gt; b GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ ∆ a,b u p ω 1 qGLYPH&lt;0&gt; ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q GLYPH&lt;16&gt; µ GLYPH&lt;0&gt; µ ∆ b,a u p ω 2 q ∆ a,b u p ω 1 q . Thus, the

point

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

the describes the highest probability that action b can occur.

(Case ii) We next consider maximizing the choice of c without any constraints. The argument follows similar reasoning to Case (i) and it omitted. The point

<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

the describes the highest probability that action c can occur.

Finally, we note that any convex combination of these choice probabilities can be induced by mixing the information structures used in these problems. Any other choice probability necessarily has a probability that is greater than either unconstrained problems. Moreover, there can be no choice distribution that only places probability on action b and c since this is not consistent with Bayesian updating. Thus, this gives a complete description of behavior consistent with Bayesian expected utility.

## References

- Sidney N Afriat. The construction of utility functions from expenditure data. International economic review , 8(1):67-77, 1967.
- Ricardo Alonso and Odilon Cˆ amara. Persuading voters. American Economic Review , 106(11):3590-3605, 2016.
- Dirk Bergemann and Stephen Morris. Bayes correlated equilibrium and the

comparison of information structures in games. Theoretical Economics , 11 (2):487-522, 2016.

- B Douglas Bernheim. Rationalizable strategic behavior. Econometrica: Journal of the Econometric Society , pages 1007-1028, 1984.
- B Douglas Bernheim and Antonio Rangel. Beyond revealed preference: choicetheoretic foundations for behavioral welfare economics. The Quarterly Journal of Economics , 124(1):51-104, 2009.
- Andrew Caplin and Mark Dean. Revealed preference, rational inattention, and costly information acquisition. The American Economic Review , 105 (7):2183-2203, 2015.
- Andrew Caplin and Daniel Martin. A testable theory of imperfect perception. The Economic Journal , 125(582):184-202, 2015.
- Andrew Caplin and Daniel Martin. Framing as information design. 2018. Working Paper.
- Christopher Chambers, Ce Liu, and John Rehbeck. Costly information acquisition. 2018. Working Paper.
- Christopher P. Chambers and John Rehbeck. Market supply with variable participants: An analysis of the u.s. cement industry from 1993-1998. 2019. Working Paper.
- Henrique de Oliveira and Rohit Lamba. Rationalizing dynamic choices. Available at SSRN , 2019.
- Tommaso Denti, Maximilian Mihm, Henrique de Oliveira, Kemal Ozbek, et al. Rationally inattentive preferences and hidden information costs. Theoretical Economics , 2016.
- Ding-Zhu Du and Panos M Pardalos. Minimax and applications , volume 4. Springer Science &amp; Business Media, 2013.

- Andrew Ellis. Foundations for optimal inattention. Journal of Economic Theory , 173:56-94, 2018.
- Haluk Ergin and Todd Sarver. A unique costly contemplation representation. Econometrica , 78(4):1285-1339, 2010.
- John D Geanakoplos. Afriat from maxmin. Economic Theory , 54(3):443-448, 2013.
- Emir Kamenica and Matthew Gentzkow. Bayesian persuasion. The American Economic Review , 101(6):2590-2615, 2011.
- YH Lin. Stochastic choice and rational inattention. Technical report, Working Paper, 2018.
- Jay Lu. Random choice and private information. Econometrica , 84(6):19832027, 2016.
- Filip Matˇ ejka and Alisdair McKay. Rational inattention to discrete choices: A new foundation for the multinomial logit model. American Economic Review , 105(1):272-98, 2015.
- Millett Granger Morgan, Max Henrion, and Mitchell Small. Uncertainty: a guide to dealing with uncertainty in quantitative risk and policy analysis . Cambridge university press, 1990.
- Stephen Morris and Philipp Strack. The wald problem and the equivalence of sequential sampling and static information costs. 2017.
- David G Pearce. Rationalizable strategic behavior and the problem of perfection. Econometrica: Journal of the Econometric Society , pages 1029-1050, 1984.
- Ralph Tyrell Rockafellar. Convex analysis . Princeton university press, 2015.
- Yuval Salant and Ariel Rubinstein. (a, f): choice with frames. The Review of Economic Studies , 75(4):1287-1296, 2008.

- Tupac Shakur. All eyez on me. Compact Disc, 1996.
- Hal R Varian. The nonparametric approach to demand analysis. Econometrica , pages 945-973, 1982.
- Abraham Wald. Statistical decision functions. The Annals of Mathematical Statistics , 20(2):165-205, 1949.
